description: Thai (Standard/Central, Bangkok) language pack for adult real-world learning. Meets the §10.1 MVP Seed Pack Target for text content in full: 300 vocabulary, 75 core phrases, 25 grammar points, 25 dialogues, 20+ slang/register items, 20 pronunciation/tone drills, 10 listening exercises, 10 writing prompts, 10 assessments, 5 roleplay scenarios. Speech grading is intentionally out of scope for this pack version (see editorialNotes).
coverage: A1-A2 everyday + adult real-world speech, built around the features that make Thai hard for English speakers: the five-tone system (mid, low, falling, high, rising) taught as a first-class 'tone guide' with pitch contours and meaning-changing minimal sets; Thai script with no inter-word spacing; the unaspirated vs aspirated stop contrast (bp/dt/g vs ph/th/kh) with no English analog; meaning-changing vowel length; the ครับ/ค่ะ politeness-particle system and the gendered, status-sensitive pronoun system as first-class social topics; and Thai classifiers. Grammar covers the analytic A1-A2 core: no conjugation/plurals/articles, topic-comment word order, tense via time words and แล้ว/จะ/กำลัง, classifiers and counting, question formation with ไหม/หรือ/question words, negation with ไม่/ยัง, the serial-verb and directional-verb patterns, comparatives, and politeness register.
editorialNotes: Standard/Central (Bangkok) Thai only — Northern (คำเมือง), Isan/Lao, and Southern variation is out of scope for this pack version (a scope decision, flagged in NATIVE_REVIEW.md, not an oversight). SPEECH GRADING IS DELIBERATELY DISABLED for Thai: no reliable off-the-shelf pronunciation scorer grades lexical tone, which is meaning-bearing, so the pronunciation surface is a display-only 'tone guide' (listen-and-repeat, ungraded) rather than a scored exercise — presenting a tone score we cannot compute accurately would mislead the learner. Romanization uses a Paiboon-style scheme with tone diacritics (mid=maa, low=maa low, falling=circumflex, high=acute, rising=caron); every tone assignment in the tone guide was derived from consonant-class + tone-mark rules but still needs a native speaker's sign-off. Identity-based slurs were deliberately EXCLUDED from the profanity set pending explicit owner review, same policy as pt-br. See NATIVE_REVIEW.md.
